critic_raw:
The dataset of 10,000 patient records collected over "the past year" introduces severe right-censoring. Readmission labels require a 30-day follow-up window post-discharge. Patients discharged in the last 30 days of the observation window have incomplete follow-up — they cannot be labeled as non-readmitted because the window has not yet elapsed. If these patients are included and labeled as non-readmitted, false negatives are systematically introduced. The design does not specify how right-censored records are handled, which is a fundamental oversight for a 30-day readmission study with only one year of data.

Wearable device data availability is not discussed as a selection bias problem. Patients who wear devices continuously for monitoring are not a random sample of post-discharge patients — they are likely younger, more health-conscious, more engaged with their care, and wealthier (device access). The design claims to compare a neural network using wearable data to logistic regression using clinical demographics, but the wearable-equipped subset may be systematically different from the general readmission population. The model may appear to perform well simply because the wearable-equipped patient cohort has lower readmission rates or more predictable risk profiles.

The feature engineering is described as "aggregation of statistics over specified windows" from wearable data — but the window specification is critical. If the aggregation window includes data from post-discharge hospital visits or from the 30-day follow-up period itself, there is post-label leakage. Heart rate and activity patterns during a readmission event would obviously predict readmission.

The design assumes temporal autocorrelation is weak without testing this assumption. Wearable-derived physiological signals (heart rate, activity) are highly autocorrelated within individuals and across days — a patient with a heart rate of 110 on day 1 post-discharge is likely to have an elevated heart rate on day 2. Random split of daily observations across train and test allows the model to learn individual patient baselines in training and apply them to held-out observations from the same individuals.

The comparison assumes the logistic regression baseline has no access to the wearable features. If the logistic regression was given engineered wearable statistics (mean heart rate, activity level) as features alongside demographics, it might perform comparably to the neural network, suggesting the gain is from the feature type, not the model architecture. The design does not test this, so architecture and feature access are again confounded.
defender_raw:
The wearable device-based readmission prediction design is innovative in feature source and makes appropriate primary metric and baseline choices, with the temporal and sample size concerns being the key open questions.

Wearable device data for post-discharge monitoring is a genuinely novel feature source for readmission prediction. Standard EHR-based models can only use pre-discharge clinical data; wearable devices provide continuous physiological monitoring in the post-discharge period, potentially capturing early signs of deterioration that precede readmission. The hypothesis that this richer temporal signal improves predictions is scientifically well-motivated.

AUROC as the primary metric is appropriate for patient risk stratification. The clinical use case — prioritizing high-risk patients for follow-up contact — requires ranking patients by readmission probability, which AUROC directly measures across operating thresholds.

The logistic regression baseline using clinical demographics is the correct comparator. It represents the conventional readmission prediction approach (clinical features available at discharge), allowing the experiment to cleanly test whether continuous wearable monitoring adds predictive value beyond what is already known at discharge.

Aggregated statistics over specified temporal windows from wearable data is the right feature engineering approach. Raw time-series from wearables at minute-level resolution would be both computationally expensive and noisy; windowed aggregates (heart rate variability over 6-hour windows, step count over 24-hour windows) are clinically interpretable and computationally tractable.

The 10,000-patient dataset over one year is modest. If the 30-day readmission rate is approximately 15%, this yields roughly 1,500 positive cases. For a neural network processing wearable time-series, this may be insufficient to learn robust patterns without strong regularization or transfer learning from pre-existing models. This is a genuine concern about whether the experiment can validly answer the hypothesis.

Socioeconomic stratification as a confound control is appropriate: SDOH factors predict readmission independently of wearable-measured physiological patterns, and controlling for them isolates the wearable signal contribution.
all_issues_raised: Right-censoring is unaddressed: patients discharged in the final 30 days of the one-year observation window cannot be correctly labeled as non-readmitted, introducing systematic false negatives.

Wearable device users are a non-random, likely healthier and wealthier subset of post-discharge patients; performance on this cohort does not generalize to the broader readmission population the hypothesis targets.
Wearable feature aggregation windows are unspecified — if they overlap with the 30-day follow-up period, physiological data from the readmission event itself leaks into features.

Temporal autocorrelation in individual physiological signals (heart rate, activity) is high; random split of daily observations places same-patient daily readings into train and test, enabling individual baseline memorization.
Logistic regression baseline lacks wearable feature access, confounding model architecture with feature type — a logistic regression on wearable summary statistics would disambiguate these.
all_issues_adjudicated: Right-censoring is unaddressed: patients discharged in the final 30 days of the one-year observation window cannot be correctly labeled as non-readmitted, introducing systematic false negatives.

Wearable device users are a non-random, likely healthier and wealthier subset of post-discharge patients; performance on this cohort does not generalize to the broader readmission population the hypothesis targets.
Wearable feature aggregation windows are unspecified — if they overlap with the 30-day follow-up period, physiological data from the readmission event itself leaks into features.
Logistic regression baseline lacks wearable feature access, confounding model architecture with feature type — a logistic regression on wearable summary statistics would disambiguate these.
